LESSONS_ADDED (0):
  Nothing novel emerged in this ticket. The Sonnet subagent thrashing on Task 3 IS noteworthy, but the existing memory-governance.md §4 THRASHING status guidance + skill BLOCKED handling already cover the response. Adding a new lesson would just restate "subagent dispatch overhead for small TDD steps can exceed Sonnet's effective budget; pivot to inline when controller has headroom" which is essentially a corollary of [[instrumentation-edge-stub-required]] / [[parallel-socket-servers-coexistence]] decisions about pragmatic adaptation. The two-stage review subagents performed correctly under their narrower scope (diff-only reads ~3-5K tokens) — that proves the model selection (Sonnet for narrow, Opus for broad) was right. Not adding a "subagent execution mode pivot" lesson because the framework rules already document it.

TIER_CLASSIFICATION: 2 — moderate. Per memory-governance §1: 4 implementation files (under 12-file Tier 3 threshold) across 2 modules with dependency depth 2. Score = (4 × 2.5) + (2 × 5) + (2 × 3) = 26 < 40 threshold → Tier 2 confirmed. Token budget: ~50K Opus 4.7 controller — well under 80K SAFE zone with comfortable margin.

PHASE_7_TICKET_13_NOTES:
  - Why dedicated `setDefaultUser` (not extension of `update`): Decision 1 — atomic, easy to unit-test, audit log captures ONLY binding changes (clean before/after), no regression risk to existing update path. The .whatsnext entry explicitly recommended dedicated mutation; the user-chosen design preview locked it in.
  - Why inline `<TableCell>` Select (not modal): Decision 2 — bindings visible at-a-glance, one-click rebind, matches atomic nature of dedicated mutation. The two-mutation-on-save choreography that would have been required by extending the edit dialog was explicitly rejected.
  - Why reuse `admin.users.list` filter (not new endpoint): Decision 3 — TanStack caches the query across pages; admin/users page next door already calls it. Server still rejects inactive as defense-in-depth.
  - Why shadcn `<Select>` (not Combobox/Command+Popover): Decision 5 — basic Select handles ~20-50 options cleanly. Combobox swap is trivial later if an org reports >50 active users.
  - Why `<DepartmentUserPicker>` as separate file (not inline): Decision 6 — each row needs its own useMutation state (isPending, error); inlining would require hook-per-row inside .map() (illegal). Keeps page.tsx from growing further (was 462 lines).
  - Why explicit BAD_REQUEST on inactive user (not just NOT_FOUND): Decision 4 — defense-in-depth for the race where a user is deactivated between dropdown render and form submit. L6 returns the user successfully (still belongs to org), but server is source of truth on whether they can be bound.
  - Why no `<DepartmentUserPicker>` unit test: Decision 7 — project posture, no jsdom/RTL added; visual QA covers UI surface. Same trade-off as Phase 7 #12's `<SpeedDialGrid>`.
  - Why subagent thrashed: Task 3 dispatched Sonnet 4.6 with a prompt containing all the inline code blocks for both the test addition and the mutation implementation (~5K prompt) plus reading 2 large existing files (departments.test.ts scaffold ~110 lines + departments.ts ~265 lines). With reasoning overhead and tool output the effective context approached/exceeded Sonnet's practical budget despite being nominally under 30K. The implementer produced correct diffs but couldn't reach the commit step. Per skill BLOCKED guidance, controller (Opus 4.7) absorbed the implementation work for remaining tasks while reviewer subagents (which only need to read commit diffs ~1-2K) stayed within their narrower scope.

  execution (intended): claude-sonnet-4-6 via Agent dispatch
